Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd (in liquidation)

Facts.Text: Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd was incorporated in Singapore as a private limited company specializing in bunker fuel supply and cargo trading.
The company was 100% owned by an ultimate holding company within an energy group.
The company operated under specialized bunker supplier and bunker craft operator licences issued by the maritime regulator.
Trade financing facilities were established with international banks to fund both bunker and cargo trading activities.
Unauthorized equipment tampering on a chartered bunker tanker was discovered during regulatory enforcement checks.
The company's primary operating licence was suspended by the regulator following the discovery of the tampering.
Formal responses to regulators and lender banks were coordinated and signed by a director acting in an executive capacity.
Between mid-June and July 2019, the company drew down approximately US$146,047,090.60 to finance purported cargo trading transactions.
The substantial drawdowns made in mid-2019 remained unpaid to the financing banks upon the company's insolvency.
An audit confirmation regarding receivables recorded as due from a major counterparty was signed by a director.
Official confirmations of indebtedness were signed and issued to lender banks in mid-2019.
A review of corporate records revealed that counterparties denied the existence of cargo trades and that bills of lading were fraudulent.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a company director is liable for corporate losses resulting from a failure to monitor a major line of business and ignoring indicators of operational and financial risk.
Party_Details.Rule: A director owes a duty to exercise reasonable skill, care, and diligence, which includes an obligation to remain informed about the company’s business and monitor management. When a company is in financial distress, directors must also manage the company with due regard for the interests of its creditors.
Party_Details.Application: Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd specialized in energy trading and incurred massive bank debt for sham transactions backed by fraudulent documents. Goh Jin Hian, categorized as an executive in internal records, failed to investigate the trading division despite signing audit confirmations for large receivables and confirmations of debt. Following a major regulatory suspension for fraud-related tampering, the director failed to scrutinize the company’s financial exposure, allowing for over US$146 million in additional drawdowns while the company was balance-sheet insolvent.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: A director's duty of care requires a minimum objective standard of supervision that cannot be delegated. Remaining ignorant of a core business line that generates the majority of a company's bank debt constitutes a breach of this duty. Internal documentation and signed financial confirmations prove the director had the opportunity and obligation to discover the irregularities. Failure to respond to indicators of risk, such as a major regulatory suspension, creates a causal link to the company's loss. Furthermore, authorizing drawdowns while the company was insolvent constituted a breach of the duty to act in the interests of creditors. The director is therefore liable for the resulting financial loss.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Goh Jin Hian

Facts.Text: Goh Jin Hian served as a director of the company from June 2011 until August 2019.
Goh Jin Hian held a 15% shareholding in the group's holding company.
Goh Jin Hian's remuneration changed from a salary to directors' fees, and CPF contributions were discontinued.
Internal company contact lists for multiple years explicitly identified Goh Jin Hian as an 'Executive Director'.
Goh Jin Hian signed an audit confirmation regarding substantial receivables recorded as due from a major trading partner.
Goh Jin Hian coordinated the company's response to the suspension of its operating licence, signing correspondence as 'Executive Director'.
Goh Jin Hian signed multiple confirmations of indebtedness to financing banks for amounts totaling approximately US$146 million.
Following resignation, Goh Jin Hian requested board resolutions describing his previous role as non-executive and seeking indemnification from corporate actions.
Goh Jin Hian communicated to authorities that he had only recently learned that bank lines were being used for cargo trading.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director may be held personally liable for a failure to supervise company operations despite an intended transition to a non-executive role and a reduction in remuneration.
Party_Details.Rule: A director’s status and duties are determined by their actual functions and the way they are held out by the company. All directors are subject to an objective standard of care to monitor corporate activities. Directors cannot delegate their primary duty of oversight to third parties or assume financial stability based solely on the continued availability of bank credit.
Party_Details.Application: Goh Jin Hian claimed to have transitioned to a non-executive role, yet remained identified as an executive in internal lists and signed critical regulatory and bank documents in that capacity. He signed confirmations for over US$140 million in debt without understanding the underlying business or the nature of the transactions. He failed to investigate clear indicators of risk, such as the suspension of operating licences for tampering, and was unaware of the company's primary source of bank-financed trading activities.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: A director's role is defined by their actual involvement and contemporaneous records rather than retroactive recharacterizations. Signing significant financial documents creates an obligation to understand the transactions being authorized. Ignorance of a primary business line constitutes a breach of the duty of care and diligence. The individual improperly delegated his supervisory responsibilities and failed to investigate indicators of financial deterioration. Consequently, he is personally liable for the corporate liabilities resulting from his lack of oversight. Relief from liability is not granted where the director's actions were not reasonable.
